outbreak.name: MV Hondius Andes hantavirus cluster
outbreak.cohort_note: Full investigated cohort of everyone who sailed on the m/v Hondius, taken from the ship company's passenger list signed by the captain (Eurosurveillance, van den Berg et al., 18 June 2026).

route.event: 29–40 passengers disembarked (operator vs. Dutch MFA discrepancy); index-case remains + widow offloaded

arrived ~10:30 AM (confirmed); 25 crew + 2 health workers disembarked in controlled phases to dockside quarantine; remains of index case removed for cremation; 23 crew in 6-week cabin quarantine aboard ship; ship disinfection completed (est. 3–4 days). After deep cleaning, Rotterdam's municipal health service (GGD) found no public-health obstacle and the MV Hondius was cleared to return to service, departing Rotterdam around 6 June 2026 bound for Svalbard (Spitsbergen).

cases.notes: Index case. Dutch national. Died aboard near Antarctic Peninsula; body preserved on board and offloaded at St. Helena on 24 Apr. No laboratory confirmation performed. WHO working hypothesis: acquired infection through environmental (rodent) exposure in Argentina before boarding.
Widow of Case 1. Disembarked at St. Helena 24 Apr, removed from KLM flight KL592 (JNB→AMS) on 25 Apr due to illness; died in South African hospital. PCR-confirmed 4 May.

British male. Medically evacuated 27 April to South Africa. PCR-confirmed 2 May. Status upgraded from critical to improving by 11 May. WHO's end-of-outbreak notice (DON611, 2 July 2026) still listed him as one of only 2 of the 10 hospitalized cases not yet discharged, with no clinical detail. The peer-reviewed investigation now answers that question: an addendum published 9 July 2026 to van den Berg et al. (Eurosurveillance) updates Table 1 to record Case 3 as recovering, and corrects his recorded age group from the 50 to 59 band to the 60 to 69 band. Earlier reporting gave his age as 69. No discharge has been announced.
Presented with pneumonia. Most recent death in the cluster (last fatality 2 May).

Developed symptoms during repatriation flight. PCR-confirmed 11 May. On life support (ECMO/artificial lung) as of 16 May; French health authorities describe as 'final stage of supportive care'. Remained on ECMO at Hôpital Bichat-Claude-Bernard (Paris). All 26 close contacts of the two French cases tested PCR-negative. The Institut Pasteur completed full-genome sequencing of the strain from the French case and found no evidence of a variant more transmissible or more dangerous than known Andes virus. The French Ministry of Health's live situation page resolves her viral status: she 'est désormais guérie de l'infection à l'hantavirus mais reste hospitalisée dans un état grave' (now cured of the hantavirus infection but still hospitalized in a serious condition), and in its entry dated 21 June 2026 states she 'reste à ce jour prise en charge en réanimation' (remains in intensive care). She has therefore cleared the virus; her continued hospitalization reflects the organ damage from severe hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome, not ongoing infection. The Eurosurveillance addendum of 9 July 2026, whose author list includes a clinician at Bichat, updates Table 1 to record Case 9 as remaining in intensive care. That is the most recent published clinical statement. No discharge has been announced.

additional_cases_note: As of 11 June 2026 the cluster totals 13 cases — now 12 confirmed and 1 probable, with 3 deaths (crude case fatality ratio 23%) (ECDC, case summary as of 11 June 2026; WHO DON604, published 28 May 2026). The total has held at 13 since 25 May; the latest change is a reclassification, not a new infection. On 10 June 2026 the United Kingdom confirmed a hantavirus case in a Tristan da Cunha islander who had been aboard the MV Hondius (boarded at Ushuaia and returned to the island), moving a previously-probable case to confirmed and raising the confirmed count from 11 to 12. The case was announced by WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus on 10 June; confirmatory testing was performed by South Africa's National Institute for Communicable Diseases (NICD). WHO continues to assess the overall public-health risk as low. The two most recent confirmed cases are a crew member who had been isolating in the Netherlands (PCR-positive 22 May, raising the total to 12) and a Spanish national already quarantining at the Gómez Ulla Military Hospital in Madrid (PCR-positive 25 May while in isolation, raising the total to 13). ECDC revised its case definition so that confirmation requires detection of Andes virus by PCR and/or serology, reclassifying the two recent cases as confirmed. No new deaths have been reported since 2 May, and no new cases since 25 May. WHO estimates the outbreak's effective reproduction number (Rt) at 0.7 as of 22 May — below the threshold for self-sustaining transmission — with a mean incubation period of 22 days and a 96% probability of safe release from quarantine at 42 days post-exposure. More than 600 contacts have now been identified across 32 countries, territories and areas (about 53% high-risk, 47% low-risk) — the first published global aggregate, and far below the 1,400–1,800 figure circulating on social media. The previously-inconclusive American case remains officially removed (negative third PCR, no antibodies detected; ECDC, 19 May 2026); the United States reports no confirmed cases. CDC repatriated 18 potentially-exposed US passengers; as of its 11 June 2026 update, 10 had returned home and 8 remained under monitoring at the UNMC/Nebraska biocontainment facility, all symptom-free (CDC had earlier issued formal quarantine orders for 2). The MV Hondius completed disinfection at Rotterdam, was cleared by the municipal health service (GGD) with no public-health obstacle, and departed Rotterdam around 6 June 2026 bound for Svalbard. A hantavirus death in Douglas County, Colorado (18 May) was confirmed by state officials as unrelated to the Hondius cluster — Sin Nombre strain, local rodent exposure, no connection to Andes virus or cruise-ship travel. June 2026 update: the total has stayed at 13 with no new infection since 25 May and no death since 2 May; the only change is the 10 June reclassification of the Tristan da Cunha case from probable to confirmed. The French passenger (Case 9) remains on ECMO at Hôpital Bichat-Claude-Bernard in Paris — still critical, no further deterioration and no resolution reported as of mid-June; the Institut Pasteur found no sign of a more transmissible or more dangerous variant. Contact monitoring continues, with WHO-cited 42-day surveillance windows running into late June / early July 2026. The first peer-reviewed case series was published in the New England Journal of Medicine (Andes Hantavirus Outbreak on a Cruise Ship, 2026; NEJMc2606496). ECDC June 17 update: some contacts have now completed their 42-day quarantine windows; the remainder are expected to do so in coming days, and the likelihood of additional cases is assessed as 'very low'. CDC June 11 update: all 18 repatriated US patients completed monitoring symptom-free — those who disembarked at St. Helena on 24 April finished their 42-day windows on 6 June. A Canadian passenger (confirmed 18 May, 70s) recovered and was discharged on 9 June. No new WHO Disease Outbreak News has been published since DON604 (28 May 2026). The outbreak is in wind-down phase with no new infection since 25 May and no new death since 2 May. June 18 2026 quarantine update (RIVM): Almost all passengers and crew of the MV Hondius ended their 42-day quarantine on 18 June 2026. Before release, every person was retested for Andes virus and all tests were negative. One person remains in quarantine longer due to close contact with a person who was hospitalized with the Andes virus. As of the CDC's most recent update (retrieved 21 June 2026), 12 of the 18 repatriated US nationals have returned home to complete monitoring and 6 remain at the National Quarantine Unit at the University of Nebraska Medical Center; all 18 remain symptom-free with no confirmed US cases. The 6 remaining NQU patients are expected to complete their 42-day windows on or around 22 June 2026 (Tenerife disembarkation 11 May + 42 days). Polymarket odds of a 2026 hantavirus pandemic fell from 5% to 4% as of 21 June 2026. June 22 2026 update: The 42-day monitoring window has now closed for all 18 US nationals at the National Quarantine Unit at the University of Nebraska Medical Center; all completed monitoring symptom-free with no confirmed US cases of Andes virus, marking the formal end of US federal quarantine operations for this outbreak. A notable public-health controversy marked the final week: HHS Secretary RFK Jr. signed a federal quarantine order on 16 June requiring Angela Perryman, a Florida resident, to remain at the NQU through 21 June, overruling a CDC medical reviewer who recommended home quarantine with telehealth monitoring. HHS cited Florida's refusal to accept the CDC's required 24/7 in-person surveillance protocol; all other states complied and their residents completed monitoring at home. Perryman's quarantine concluded on 21 June and was released on 23 June. No new infections or deaths have been reported since 25 May and 2 May respectively. June 23 2026 update: HHS Secretary Kennedy formally announced the successful conclusion of the US federal public health response. More than 100 CDC staff who had been deployed at the peak of the response were freed from the incident management assignment. The US monitoring effort ended with zero confirmed hantavirus cases among all 18 repatriated nationals. June 24 2026 update: WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus stated that the outbreak will be formally declared over on 2 July 2026 if no new cases are reported by that date. As of the WHO announcement: the cluster total remains at 13 cases (12 confirmed, 1 probable) and 3 deaths; all but 54 of the identified contacts have completed their quarantine, with the remaining 54 expected to finish by 2 July; more than 650 contacts have now been identified across 33 countries, territories and areas (up from 600+ across 32 as of WHO DON604). The French patient (Case 9) remains on ECMO at Hôpital Bichat-Claude-Bernard in Paris with no confirmed outcome reported as of 28 June 2026. Polymarket odds for a 2026 hantavirus pandemic hold at 4% as of 27 June 2026. The outbreak is approaching formal closure pending the WHO July 2 declaration. June 29 2026 update: all 18 US nationals who were monitored at the National Quarantine Unit at the University of Nebraska Medical Center are confirmed home, completing symptom-free monitoring with zero US cases of Andes virus (Medical Daily, June 29 2026; CBC). Canadian contacts who had been linked to the MV Hondius voyage completed their isolation periods, with the final Canadian contacts clearing quarantine on or around June 29 (CBC). June 30 2026: the Polymarket 'Hantavirus outbreak by June 30?' prediction market resolved NO at 1% yes — consistent with the official WHO, ECDC, and CDC assessments that no broader outbreak occurred. The French patient (Case 9) remains in ICU on ECMO at Hôpital Bichat-Claude-Bernard in Paris with no confirmed outcome change as of June 30 2026. July 2 2026: the World Health Organization formally declared the outbreak over, after the last of the identified close contacts was released from quarantine with no new cases reported since 25 May. The final tally stands at 13 cases (12 confirmed, 1 probable) and 3 deaths (crude case fatality ratio 23%), with more than 650 contacts traced across 33 countries, territories and areas. July 4 2026 update: WHO's formal end-of-outbreak notice (DON611, published 2 July 2026) supersedes DON604 as the definitive closing record and adds precise figures not previously published. Contact tracing closed at 653 total contacts across 33 countries, territories and areas — 317 high-risk contacts completed quarantine and monitoring, and 336 low-risk contacts completed self-monitoring — with the 42-day follow-up period concluding with no additional secondary cases detected. Demographically, of the 13 cases 9 were male and 4 female, with a median age of 65 (IQR 56–70). Of the 10 cases who were hospitalized, 8 have been discharged; 2 remain hospitalized — the British patient in South Africa (Case 3) and the French patient on ECMO in Paris (Case 9) — neither with a further clinical update reported. DON611 states the outbreak 'no longer poses a public health risk and no further related transmission is expected.' Polymarket's 'Hantavirus pandemic in 2026?' market ticked up slightly to 5% yes as of 4 July 2026 (from 4% in late June), which reflects normal market noise rather than any new epidemiological signal — WHO, ECDC, and CDC all maintain their low-risk assessments. This tracker will remain live as a historical record; no further routine updates are expected absent new developments (e.g. a change in the French or South African patients' status). July 9 2026 update: no epidemiological change in the week since WHO closed the outbreak. The count holds at 13 cases (12 confirmed, 1 probable) and 3 deaths, with no new infection since 25 May and no new death since 2 May. WHO has published no further Disease Outbreak News on this outbreak after DON611 (2 July 2026); the next entry in the series, DON612 (3 July 2026), concerns Bundibugyo virus disease in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda and is unrelated to the MV Hondius cluster. ECDC's outbreak page still carries its 17 June 2026 epidemiological update; and South Africa's NICD still lists 2 cases hospitalized (one in South Africa, one in France) as of 2 July. Neither the British patient in South Africa (Case 3) nor the French patient on ECMO in Paris (Case 9) has had a clinical outcome published, so both remain open questions on this page. The only movement this week is in the prediction markets. Polymarket's 'Hantavirus pandemic in 2026?' fell from 5% to about 2% yes (trading at 2.25 cents on 9 July 2026), reversing the 4 July uptick and settling back to the level implied by the official low-risk assessments. The 'Hantavirus vaccine in 2026?' market eased from 7% to about 6%. 'Hantavirus lab leak confirmed by June 30?' has now resolved NO, joining the June 30 outbreak market; no lab-leak origin was ever substantiated by any WHO, ECDC, or CDC document. Verified 9 July 2026 against the French Ministry of Health's live situation page: the French patient (Case 9) is now cured of the hantavirus infection itself but remains hospitalized in a serious condition, and as of the ministry's 21 June 2026 entry remains in intensive care. Her continued hospitalization reflects organ damage from severe hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome rather than ongoing infection; no discharge has been announced. No comparable update has been published for the British patient in South Africa (Case 3). A source conflict on the passenger/crew split (WHO: 88/59; CDC: 86/61, both totalling 147) is documented in the fact-check section; at that time this tracker followed WHO, a policy since superseded by the peer-reviewed cohort figure below. July 10 2026 update: the two open clinical questions on this page are now answered in the peer-reviewed record. Eurosurveillance published the multi-country outbreak investigation (van den Berg et al., 18 June 2026, DOI 10.2807/1560-7917.ES.2026.31.24.2600477), and on 9 July 2026 the journal issued an addendum at the authors' request. The addendum updates Table 1 to record Case 3, the British patient evacuated to South Africa, as recovering, and Case 9, the French patient in Paris, as remaining in intensive care; Case 10 is recorded as recovered. It corrects Case 3's age group from the 50 to 59 band to the 60 to 69 band, corrects the confirmation date for Case 11 to 16 May and for Case 12 to 20 May, and confirms that all quarantines have been lifted with no additional cases found, the last extended quarantine for a single close contact having ended on 2 July. The addendum also adds a notable laboratory finding: infectious Andes virus was isolated from a blood sample drawn 5 days before that patient developed symptoms, during weekly testing of quarantined contacts. The sample gave an RT-PCR quantification cycle value of 23 and was negative for both IgM and IgG, confirming that live virus can be recovered before any antibody response. The paper reports that the outbreak strain is closely related to the Andes virus behind the 2018 person to person outbreak in Epuyén, Argentina, while cautioning that the genomic finding is preliminary. The investigation also resolves the passenger and crew arithmetic that WHO and CDC left inconsistent: the full cohort under investigation is 182 people, 121 passengers and 61 crew, from the captain-signed passenger list, whereas the 147 both agencies cite is a mid-voyage headcount. This page now shows 182 as the headline complement. Nothing else has moved: France's Ministry of Health live page carries no entry newer than 2 July and still describes the French patient as cured of the infection but hospitalized in a serious condition; South Africa's NICD page is unchanged since 2 July and still lists 2 cases hospitalized; and no WHO Disease Outbreak News after DON611 concerns this outbreak. Polymarket's 'Hantavirus pandemic in 2026?' market trades near 2% yes and the vaccine market near 6%; the June 30 outbreak market and the lab leak market have both settled NO.

incubation_period.safe_release: WHO estimates a 96% probability of safe release from quarantine at 42 days post-exposure (DON604).
incubation_period.note: The 4–42 day range has been the CDC's documented incubation period for Andes virus since the 1990s. WHO's most recent guidance gives a mean of 22 days, a typical range of 1–6 weeks (i.e. 7–42 days), and a potential range of 1–8 weeks.
incubation_period.presymptomatic: Infectious Andes virus was isolated from a blood sample drawn 5 days before that patient's symptoms began, during weekly testing of contacts in quarantine. The sample gave an ANDV-specific RT-PCR quantification cycle value of 23 and was negative for both IgM and IgG antibodies (Eurosurveillance addendum, 9 July 2026). Live virus can therefore be recovered before any antibody response, which is why quarantine rather than symptom screening alone was used to contain the cluster.

contacts_traced.summary: WHO's end-of-outbreak notice (DON611, 2 July 2026) gives the final, precise contact-tracing tally: 653 contacts across 33 countries, territories and areas — 317 high-risk contacts who completed quarantine and monitoring, and 336 low-risk contacts who completed self-monitoring. This supersedes the earlier 'more than 650' estimate (WHO DG Tedros, 24 June 2026) and remains far below the 1,400–1,800 figure circulating on social media, which no WHO, ECDC, or CDC document supports. The peer-reviewed investigation reports a smaller number, 188 high-risk contacts as at 18 June 2026, and its 9 July 2026 addendum states explicitly that this is the aggregate number of people placed in quarantine by the seven countries that reported cases among their own nationals. It is a subset of the global 653, not a competing total. The 42-day follow-up period concluded with no additional secondary cases detected, and WHO states the outbreak "no longer poses a public health risk and no further related transmission is expected."
contacts_traced.st_helena_disembarked: 32 passengers, 22 to 24 April (Eurosurveillance, van den Berg et al.). Earlier counts of 29 (Oceanwide Expeditions) and about 40 (Dutch Foreign Ministry) are superseded by the published investigation.
contacts_traced.st_helena_high_risk: small number of higher-risk contacts isolating for 45 days
contacts_traced.united_states: All 18 repatriated passengers at the National Quarantine Unit (NQU) at the University of Nebraska Medical Center completed monitoring symptom-free and are confirmed home as of June 29 2026. Passengers who disembarked at St. Helena on 24 April finished their 42-day period on 6 June. The final 6 NQU patients completed their 42-day windows on 22 June 2026 (Tenerife disembarkation 11 May + 42 days). Notable: HHS Secretary RFK Jr. signed a federal quarantine order on 16 June requiring Angela Perryman (Florida) to remain at the NQU through 21 June, overruling a CDC medical reviewer who recommended home quarantine. HHS cited Florida's refusal to accept the CDC's required 24/7 in-person surveillance; all 17 other passengers were permitted home quarantine once their states complied. The CDC formally concluded its federal hantavirus response on 24 June 2026, releasing more than 100 deployed staff. No confirmed US cases of Andes virus.
contacts_traced.united_kingdom: 2 passengers self-isolating plus a small number of secondary contacts
contacts_traced.south_africa: Contacts from KLM flight KL592 (88 people aboard) traced and cleared. Case 3 (British male, med-evac'd 27 April) remains hospitalized in South Africa and is recorded as recovering in the Eurosurveillance addendum of 9 July 2026.
contacts_traced.limitations: Some passengers who disembarked at St. Helena returned home before contact tracing was established. WHO acknowledges tracing is incomplete, but with the effective reproduction number (Rt) estimated at 0.7 as of 22 May and no secondary cases identified after disembarkation, wider-population risk is assessed as 'extremely low'. On 18 June 2026, almost all passengers and crew ended their 42-day quarantine; RIVM confirmed every person retested negative before release. One person remains in quarantine longer due to close contact with a hospitalized patient. A total of 34 Canadians completed monitoring: 9 high-risk (mandatory self-isolation), 26 low-risk (monitored after sharing flights), and 1 confirmed case (Yukon, recovered and discharged 9 June 2026). All 34 completed their monitoring periods by 29 June 2026 (CBC, Global News). Andes virus typically requires close, prolonged contact for human-to-human transmission. WHO's end-of-outbreak notice (DON611, 2 July 2026) confirms the final tally: 317 high-risk contacts completed quarantine/monitoring and 336 low-risk contacts completed self-monitoring (653 total), with the 42-day follow-up period closing with no additional secondary cases detected — 'this outbreak no longer poses a public health risk and no further related transmission is expected.'

clarifications.claim: WHO quietly changed the incubation period to 'as early as 4 days'
clarifications.detail: The 4-day lower bound (range 4–42 days) is the long-standing CDC figure, in place since the 1990s. WHO's most recent guidance (DON604, 28 May 2026) gives a mean incubation of 22 days and a 96% probability of safe release at 42 days; its earlier DON601 gives a typical range of 1–6 weeks (minimum 1 week, not 4 days). No 'quiet change' occurred.

clarifications.claim: 1,400 to 1,800 people are unknowingly exposed and walking around
clarifications.detail: WHO's end-of-outbreak notice (DON611, 2 July 2026) gives the final global tally: 653 contacts across 33 countries, territories and areas, made up of 317 high-risk contacts and 336 low-risk contacts. That is the complete aggregate and it is well below 1,400. The ship itself carried 182 people, 121 passengers and 61 crew. The 1,400 to 1,800 number appears to be a social-media estimate, not an official one, and no WHO, ECDC, or CDC document publishes such a figure.
sources: Eurosurveillance (van den Berg et al., 18 Jun 2026)

clarifications.claim: Authorities can't confirm if 24 or 40 passengers got off the ship at St. Helena
clarifications.detail: The peer-reviewed investigation settles this. Eurosurveillance (van den Berg et al., 18 June 2026) records that the m/v Hondius lay at Saint Helena from 22 to 24 April, where 32 passengers disembarked, among them the body of Case 1 and Case 2, the partner of Case 1. One crew member embarked there, and Case 3 went ashore and reboarded. The earlier figures of 29 (Oceanwide Expeditions) and about 40 (Dutch Foreign Ministry) are superseded. The 24 figure appears in no source we found.
sources: Eurosurveillance (van den Berg et al., 18 Jun 2026)

clarifications.claim: A new, invisible chain of community transmission is about to start
clarifications.detail: Andes virus is the only hantavirus with documented human-to-human transmission, but transmission typically requires close, prolonged contact (household, intensive caregiving). WHO estimates the effective reproduction number (Rt) at 0.7 as of 22 May — below the threshold for self-sustaining spread — and no secondary (post-disembarkation) cases have been identified as of late May 2026. WHO and ECDC both assess wider-population risk as 'extremely low'.

clarifications.claim: A Colorado hantavirus death is part of the Hondius outbreak
clarifications.detail: A Douglas County, Colorado adult died of hantavirus on May 18, 2026. Colorado state health officials and the CDC confirmed this case is completely unrelated to the MV Hondius cluster. The Colorado case involves Sin Nombre hantavirus (the strain endemic to North American deer mice), which does NOT spread person-to-person. The Hondius cluster is Andes virus, a separate South American strain. The Colorado patient had no known exposure to the cruise ship or its passengers.

clarifications.claim: FIFA games / mass events will accelerate spread
clarifications.detail: Andes virus is not influenza or SARS-CoV-2; it does not spread efficiently in casual crowd settings. Documented person-to-person transmission has only occurred among close household and healthcare contacts of symptomatic patients. There is no public-health basis for linking FIFA or similar mass gatherings to outbreak amplification.

clarifications.claim: The MV Hondius carried 86 passengers and 61 crew
clarifications.detail: Both agencies were splitting a mid-voyage headcount, not the ship's complement. The peer-reviewed investigation (van den Berg et al., Eurosurveillance, 18 June 2026, amended 9 July 2026) puts the full investigated cohort at 182 people, 121 passengers and 61 crew, taken from the ship company's passenger list signed by the captain. WHO DON599 (4 May 2026) reports 147 individuals aboard, 88 passengers and 59 crew, with the vessel moored off Cabo Verde, by which point 32 passengers had already left at Saint Helena. Eurosurveillance separately records 147 aboard on arrival at Tenerife on 10 May, made up of 83 passengers, 60 crew and a medical team of four that had boarded at Praia on 6 May. CDC HAN00528 gives 147 as 86 passengers and 61 crew. The WHO and CDC splits still differ from one another and neither agency has reconciled them, but both describe a reduced ship population partway through the voyage. This tracker now reports the 182 person cohort as its headline figure and treats 147 as a snapshot.
sources: Eurosurveillance (van den Berg et al., 18 Jun 2026)

clarifications.claim: Only 188 people were ever traced, so WHO's 653 contact figure is inflated
clarifications.detail: The two numbers count different populations and both are correct. Eurosurveillance reports 188 high-risk contacts as at 18 June 2026, and its 9 July 2026 addendum spells out that this is the aggregate number of people placed in quarantine by the seven countries that reported cases among their own nationals. WHO DON611 reports 653 contacts in total across 33 countries, territories and areas: 317 high-risk contacts who completed quarantine and monitoring, and 336 low-risk contacts who completed self-monitoring. The 188 figure is a subset limited to seven countries, not a global total.

secondary.market: Hantavirus lab leak confirmed by June 30?
